narrative_blocks.header: She buried seven children and lived to ninety
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Beers married John Pierpont and bore ten children, but only four reached adulthood; she named three daughters Sarah and lost two as infants and the third at fourteen. She lived to ninety, outliving her husband by thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: The British seized her home as a hospital
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1779 British invasion of New Haven, soldiers raided the house her husband John Pierpont had built and took it for use as a hospital and headquarters. The home stood at the center of the town's worst wartime days.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother guarded the captured spy Major André
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah's brother, Captain Nathan Beers, commanded the guard over the British spy Major John André at his trial and execution in 1780. André's hanging, in the wake of Benedict Arnold's treason, was among the most talked-of events of the war.

narrative_blocks.header: She buried seven children and lived to ninety
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Beers married John Pierpont and bore ten children, but only four reached adulthood; she named three daughters Sarah and lost two as infants and the third at fourteen. She lived to ninety, outliving her husband by thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: The British seized her home as a hospital
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1779 British invasion of New Haven, soldiers raided the house her husband John Pierpont had built and took it for use as a hospital and headquarters. The home stood at the center of the town's worst wartime days.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother guarded the captured spy Major André
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah's brother, Captain Nathan Beers, commanded the guard over the British spy Major John André at his trial and execution in 1780. André's hanging, in the wake of Benedict Arnold's treason, was among the most talked-of events of the war.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

H00388.prominent_name_reason: John Pierpont (1740-1805) — great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan (1837-1913). Chain: John Pierpont (H00388) → James Pierpont → John Pierpont the poet (1785-1866, abolitionist) → Juliet Pierpont × Junius Spencer Morgan → John Pierpont Morgan. J.P. Morgan's middle name "Pierpont" commemorates this descent from the Pierpont family of New Haven — founded by Rev. James Pierpont, who married Mary Hooker (H00034). Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-granddaughter's grandson became the most powerful financier in American history.

bio.notes: Sources also give surname as "Pierrepont" — alternate spelling

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandson was John Pierpont Morgan (1837-1913), the financier whose name 'J.P. Morgan' carries forward this Pierpont line. Through his son James Pierpont and grandson John Pierpont the poet, the name traveled four generations to one of America's most consequential bankers.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived in old Pierpont mansion, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight described him as 'a large landholder who lived in the old family-mansion at New Haven.' He had ten children, of whom only four reached adulthood and only three married.

narrative_blocks.header: Family later restored 'Pierrepont' French spelling
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 'Pierpont' but late in his life, around 1802, Hezekiah Beers Pierpont requested the family restore the original French 'Pierrepont' spelling. Hezekiah did not adopt it himself for business reasons; descendants split between the two spellings thereafter.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

narrative_blocks.header: Aged Brooklyn gin a full year for mellowness
narrative_blocks.body: In 1801 he bought Philip Livingston's old brewery on the Brooklyn waterfront and converted it to the Anchor Gin Distillery. The site closed in 1819 and is now part of One Brooklyn Bridge Park.

narrative_blocks.header: Half a million acres at eight cents apiece
narrative_blocks.body: His 1802 marriage to Anna Maria Constable made him heir to her father William Constable's Northern New York holdings. The land had been bought decades earlier when there was no buyer for it.

narrative_blocks.header: Proposed the Brooklyn Heights Promenade
narrative_blocks.body: He sketched a public walkway along the bluffs above the harbor more than a century before it was built. The Promenade as it exists today follows the route he proposed.

narrative_blocks.header: Pirates seized his ship and bankrupted him
narrative_blocks.body: The Confederacy was taken at sea in 1797 carrying his firm's cargo. The loss ended his Paris-based mercantile career and forced him home to start again.

narrative_blocks.header: Witnessed Robespierre's execution from the crowd
narrative_blocks.body: He spent seven years in France during the 1790s tending his firm's shipments. He was in Paris for the Reign of Terror and saw the head of the Jacobin regime guillotined.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

cross_connections.display_label: Henry Evelyn Pierrepont co-founded the American Geographical Society (1851) alongside.

narrative_blocks.header: Designated the land that became Green-Wood
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed in 1833 to lay out the streets of Brooklyn, he set aside the glacial moraine above Gowanus Creek for what would become America's most influential rural cemetery. He co-founded Green-Wood and served as its president from 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: His Brooklyn street plan included eleven parks
narrative_blocks.body: He drew the grid of the newly incorporated City of Brooklyn while traveling in Europe in 1833. Some historians call him one of the first city planners in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Monopolized East River ferries until the bridge
narrative_blocks.body: In 1844 he merged five operators into the Brooklyn Union Ferry Company. The monopoly held for nearly forty years until the Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883.

narrative_blocks.header: Owned a Stuart Washington portrait Lafayette authenticated
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather William Constable commissioned the painting from Gilbert Stuart in 1797 as a duplicate of the Lansdowne. Lafayette saw it in 1824 and said 'That is really my old friend indeed.'

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded the American Geographical Society in 1851
narrative_blocks.body: He served on its founding council for ten years and was first president of the Brooklyn Academy of Music. Pierrepont Street in Brooklyn Heights still carries the family name.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

notable.notable_blurb: Pierrepont Manor namesake; Jefferson County railroad president

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the three-story 26-room Manor House in 1826 on 7.17 acres; the hamlet itself was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: Met every train wearing cape with small dog
narrative_blocks.body: From May 28, 1851 — the day the first railroad train steamed into the hamlet of Pierrepont Manor — almost to the day of his death December 20, 1885, William Constable Pierrepont met every train that passed through that community, always with watch in hand to check the scheduled time. He was a little man wearing a long cape and followed by a small dog. The thirty-four-year ritual of standing trackside at every train arrival was the defining public habit of his second half of life.

narrative_blocks.header: Managed one-million-acre Pierrepont land empire
narrative_blocks.body: From about 1820 — when he was seventeen — William Constable Pierrepont moved to Bear Creek New York to manage his family's roughly one-million-acre landholdings in Jefferson, Lewis, and Saint Lawrence Counties of upstate New York. The town of Bear Creek was renamed Pierrepont Manor in his honor. He built both the Pierrepont Manor house and the Land Office — buildings now on the National Register of Historic Places.

narrative_blocks.header: President Watertown & Rome Railroad first into Jefferson
narrative_blocks.body: On April 10, 1851, William Constable Pierrepont was elected president of the Watertown & Rome Railroad — the elections precisely timed with the railroad's arrival at Pierrepont Manor on May 28, 1851. That arrival was the first railroad train into Jefferson County New York. He simultaneously founded and endowed Zion Episcopal Church at Pierrepont Manor in 1835 — the small village's denominational anchor for the rest of the century.

narrative_blocks.header: Built 26-room Manor house and Zion Episcopal Church
narrative_blocks.body: In 1826 William Constable Pierrepont built his three-story 26-room frame Manor House on seven and a sevenths acres — an impressive structure with tall pillars and a colonial doorway with leaded glass transome and sidelights, sitting back from the road in a picturesque grove. Ten years later in 1836 he built Zion Episcopal Church across the street from the Manor house and Land Office; the church served the community he had named and given form to.

narrative_blocks.header: Four-term Town supervisor; one-term State Assemblyman
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont served as Town of Ellisburg supervisor in 1840, 1843, 1846, and 1847 — four non-consecutive terms across the decade — and as New York State Assemblyman in 1840-1841. The civic offices ran in parallel with his ongoing land-management business, his railroad presidency from 1851, and his Zion Episcopal Church patronage. His personal wealth at the time was estimated at five hundred thousand dollars.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with Astronomer Royal about Great Pyramid
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont's intellectual interests ranged far beyond land management. He corresponded with the Astronomer Royal Professor Charles Piazzi Smyth about Smyth's controversial calculations on the Great Pyramid of Giza — the mid-Victorian astronomical-archaeological project that attempted to derive sacred-measurement units from pyramid geometry. His own maps at age seventy-six were described by contemporaries as 'marvels of accuracy.' He kept sixty-two years of daily-weather and land-sale diaries from 1823 through 1885 — the archive now at Cornell University Library, Collection 1094.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest of Hezekiah Pierrepont; five daughters, two sons
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont was born October 3, 1803 in New York City, eldest son of Hezekiah Beers Pierrepont and Anna Maria Constable — making him a seventh-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker. He had seven children, five daughters and two sons (both sons died young), and died December 20, 1885 at Pierrepont Manor at age eighty-two, buried in Pierrepont Manor Cemetery. The Constable-Pierrepont Papers covering 1762-1911 are at Columbia University Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Caroline died young
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline died in 1823, aged fifteen or sixteen.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named for Robert Fulton, inventor of the steamboat, personal friend of H00976 Hezekiah Beers Pierrepont.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Filed Virginia's first emancipation bill

narrative_blocks.header: Sarah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died in 1788, aged about fourteen.

orbit_connections.name: Roger Sherman Baldwin (Connecticut Governor)
orbit_connections.relationship: Governor Baldwin was married to a former student of Herrick's school for young women in New Haven
orbit_connections.notes: Rev. Claudius Herrick founded a school for young women in New Haven in 1808, running it until his death in 1831. Half-price tuition for daughters of clergymen. Wikipedia: "Three Episcopal bishops and a Connecticut governor, Roger Sherman Baldwin, were married to former students of the school." Roger Sherman Baldwin (1793-1863) was Governor of Connecticut 1844-1846 and US Senator 1847-1851. He was also the defense attorney in the Amistad case (1839), securing freedom for the enslaved Africans who had seized the ship. Named for his grandfather Roger Sherman, who signed both the Declaration of Independence and the US Constitution. (Wikipedia on Claudius Herrick; Wikipedia on Roger Sherman Baldwin)

notable.notable_blurb: Minister who founded a New Haven girls' school

narrative_blocks.header: He founded a New Haven school for women
narrative_blocks.body: After ill health ended his Woodbridge pastorate, Claudius Herrick opened a school for young women in New Haven that ran from 1808 until his death. He charged daughters of clergymen half the usual tuition.

narrative_blocks.header: His pupils married bishops and a governor
narrative_blocks.body: Three Episcopal bishops and Governor Roger Sherman Baldwin all married former students of his school. Herrick himself had earlier served as head of Deerfield Academy.

notable.notable_blurb: First full-time librarian of Yale College

narrative_blocks.header: He proved the August meteors return yearly
narrative_blocks.detail: Son of Rev. Claudius Herrick (X00500) and Hannah Pierpont (H00979). Born Feb 24, 1811 in New Haven CT; died Jun 11, 1862 age 51. Yale librarian and treasurer. Made careful observations of meteor showers and contributed to natural history research. Never married; cared for his widowed mother Hannah Pierpont Herrick until her death in 1859, two years before his own death. His father's Wikipedia page notes: "He was the father of scientist Edward Claudius Herrick." (Wikipedia on Claudius Herrick; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1839 Herrick demonstrated the annual periodicity of the Perseid meteor shower — among the first to establish it scientifically — while serving as Yale’s librarian and treasurer. He published on harvesting ants besides.

narrative_blocks.header: He stayed home with his mother
narrative_blocks.body: Herrick never married, keeping house with his widowed mother Hannah Pierpont Herrick until her death in 1859 — and outliving her by only three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale’s librarian never went to college
narrative_blocks.body: He liked to say he “graduated at Father Swift’s school, in Derby” at about thirteen; from the counter of Hezekiah Howe’s famous bookstore, Yale made him its first full-time Librarian in 1843 and its Treasurer in 1852. He declined a place on the Wilkes Exploring Expedition.

cross_connections.display_label: gave Miami its library, a century after him
was her mother-in-law; Sister of cotton-gin inventor Eli Whitney

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two children to disease in early 1836
narrative_blocks.body: In early 1836 Eliza Maria buried two of her young children within eight weeks — six-year-old Robert Pierrepont (HD1569) on January 17 and two-year-old Eliza Maria (HD1571) on March 13. She gave the name Eliza Maria again to a daughter born five years later (HD1576), passing down the name from the daughter she had lost.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named to honor the Pierpont/Pierrepont branch of mother Mary Pierpont Foster (H00981).
Mother Mary Pierpont Foster (H00981) chose "Hooker" as middle name, honoring the Pierpont-Hooker ancestry, gen 7 from Thomas Hooker.
Replacement naming: first child Edward William Foster (HD1558) died in infancy 1808.
Named for maternal grandfather Nathan Beers, father of Sarah Beers Pierpont (X00494).

sources.short: EH 1909; Wikipedia on Benjamin Tallmadge; Wikipedia on Major André
